details.gloss: Fourth Crusade forces captured and sacked Constantinople, creating the Latin Empire. The event fractured Byzantine power and reshaped the eastern Mediterranean.

image.alt: Historical image associated with Siege of Masada: Israel 2013 Aerial 21 Masada

details.gloss: Roman forces under Lucius Flavius Silva besieged and captured the desert fortress of Masada, the last major rebel holdout of the First Jewish-Roman War.
details.casualties: Sicarii and inhabitants: about 960 dead; Roman Empire: not recorded

image.alt: Historical image associated with Siege of Busanjin: Siege of Busanjin 1592

details.gloss: Japanese forces captured Busanjin in the opening battle of the invasions of Korea. The fall of Busan opened the route for Japan's rapid advance into Joseon.
details.casualties: Japan: not recorded; Joseon Korea: about 1,200-8,500 killed and 200 captured

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Okpo: Okpo battle monument

details.gloss: Yi Sun-sin and Won Kyun destroyed Japanese transports at Okpo. It was Yi's first naval victory and began the pressure on Japanese sea logistics.
details.casualties: Toyotomi Japan: 26 transports lost; Joseon Korea: 1 wounded

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Hansan Island: Documentary painting of the Battle of Hansan Island
image.attribution: War Memorial of Korea Open Archives

details.gloss: Yi Sun-sin's fleet defeated Japanese naval forces near Hansan Island and Angolpo. The victory sharply limited Japanese operations along Korea's southern coast.
details.casualties: Toyotomi Japan: about 100 ships lost; Joseon Korea: 19 dead and 114 wounded

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Haengju: Haengjudaecheopbi

details.gloss: Gwon Yul's smaller Joseon force held Haengjusanseong against repeated Japanese attacks. The victory threatened Japanese positions around Hanseong.
details.casualties: Joseon Korea: not recorded; Toyotomi Japan: at least 110 casualties, possibly much higher

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Bibracte: Divico und Caesar

details.gloss: Caesar defeated the Helvetii-led migration near Bibracte, halting their movement through Gaul. It was the first major battle of the Gallic Wars and justified deeper Roman intervention.
details.casualties: Roman Republic: unknown; Helvetii-led coalition: 238,000 killed or captured according to Caesar

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Vosges: Battaglia Ariovisto Mulhouse png

details.gloss: Caesar defeated Ariovistus and his Germanic coalition near the Vosges. The victory pushed Germanic forces back toward the Rhine and deepened Roman intervention in Gaul.
details.casualties: Roman Republic: unknown; Suebi and allies: 80,000 killed according to Caesar

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of the Sabis: SamberMerbesleChâteau

details.location: Near the Sabis River, northern Gaul
details.gloss: The Nervii and allied Belgic tribes ambushed Caesar's army near the Sabis. Roman discipline and reinforcements turned a near disaster into a victory.
details.casualties: Roman Republic: unknown; Nervii-led Belgae: about 59,500 casualties

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Morbihan: Battaglia Veneti 56 aC

details.gloss: Decimus Brutus defeated the Veneti fleet off Brittany after Roman crews disabled their rigging. The victory broke Venetic naval power and secured Caesar's coastal campaign.
details.casualties: Roman Republic: light losses; Veneti: almost all ships destroyed or captured

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Gergovia: L’oppidum de Gergovie, vestiges sur le plateau

details.gloss: Caesar's attempt to storm Vercingetorix's stronghold at Gergovia collapsed after confusion near the walls. The defeat forced Caesar to retreat before the later campaign at Alesia.
details.casualties: Roman Republic and allies: at least 46 centurions and 700 legionaries killed; modern estimates several thousand killed; Gallic tribes: unknown

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Heiligerlee: Slag bij heiligerlee

details.gloss: Louis of Nassau defeated a Spanish force at Heiligerlee, the first Dutch rebel battlefield victory of the Eighty Years War. Adolf of Nassau was killed in the action.
details.casualties: Dutch rebels: 50 dead or wounded; Spanish Empire: 460 dead

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Jemmingen: Slag bij Jemmingen, 1568, RP P 2016 1302 32

details.gloss: The Duke of Alba crushed Louis of Nassau's rebel army at Jemmingen after the failed attempt to take Groningen. The defeat erased the momentum from Heiligerlee.
details.casualties: Dutch rebels: 7,000 dead or wounded; Spanish Empire: 8 dead and 220 wounded

image.alt: Historical image associated with Siege of Haarlem: Kenau Hasselaar op de wallen van Haarlem

details.gloss: Haarlem surrendered after a bloody Spanish siege, and its garrison was massacred. The defense became a model of resistance for later Dutch rebel strongholds.
details.casualties: Dutch rebels and relief forces: about 2,700-7,000 dead or wounded; Spain: at least 1,700 dead plus thousands of casualties

image.alt: Historical image associated with Siege of Leiden: Veen01

details.gloss: Spanish forces failed to capture Leiden after Dutch rebels flooded the surrounding polders and relieved the city. The victory became one of the revolt's defining episodes.
details.casualties: Dutch rebels: about 500 casualties; Spain: about 2,000 casualties

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Mookerheyde: Slag op de Moockerheijde
image.attribution: Frans van den Wijngaerde, after Johann Wilhelm Baur

details.gloss: Spanish tercios defeated a Dutch rebel mercenary army near Mook. Louis and Henry of Nassau were killed, weakening William the Silent's campaign.
details.casualties: Dutch rebels: about 3,000 dead or wounded; Spain: about 150 dead or wounded

image.alt: Historical image associated with Fall of Antwerp: Parma's brug, Antwerpen 1585

details.gloss: Alessandro Farnese captured Antwerp after a long siege and blockade of the Scheldt. The fall transformed trade in the Low Countries and accelerated migration north.
details.casualties: States-General: about 8,000 casualties; Spain: about 1,800 casualties

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Turnhout: De slagh bij Turnhout in den jaere MDXCVII 1597 (Jan Luyken, 1681)

details.gloss: Maurice of Nassau and Francis Vere caught a Spanish column near Turnhout. Anglo-Dutch cavalry broke the Spanish infantry in a sharp demonstration of Dutch reforms.
details.casualties: Spanish Empire: about 2,000 killed or wounded and 500-700 captured; Dutch Republic and England: 12 killed and 50 wounded

image.alt: Historical image associated with Siege of 's-Hertogenbosch: Spanish troops retreat after the siege of Den Bosch in 1629

details.gloss: Frederick Henry captured the major Spanish stronghold of 's-Hertogenbosch after an ambitious siege and drainage operation. The victory strengthened Dutch control in Brabant.
details.casualties: Spain: 1,200 killed and 1,200 diseased or injured; Dutch Republic: unknown

image.alt: Historical image associated with Fall of Granada: La Rendición de Granada Pradilla

combatants: Catholic Monarchs of Castile and Aragon
details.gloss: Granada surrendered to Ferdinand and Isabella after the final campaigns against the Nasrid emirate. The capitulation ended Muslim rule in Iberia and closed the Reconquista.

image.alt: Historical image associated with Siege of the Alcazar: FP Toledo Alcazar 2025 Views

details.gloss: Nationalist cadets, Civil Guards, and allies held the Alcazar of Toledo against Republican militia until Franco's Army of Africa relieved it. The siege became an early propaganda victory for the Nationalists.

image.alt: Historical image associated with Siege of Madrid: Nationalist soldiers raiding Madrid, March 1937
image.attribution: Concern Illustrated Daily Courier - Illustration Archive

details.gloss: Nationalist forces tried to seize Madrid in late 1936, but Republican defenders and International Brigades held the capital. The failed assault turned the city into a long siege until March 1939.

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Jarama: Savoia Marchetti SM.81

details.gloss: Nationalist forces attacked east of Madrid to cut the road to Valencia. Fierce fighting, including heavy International Brigade losses, ended without the breakthrough Franco sought.

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Guadalajara: Bundesarchiv Bild 183 2006 1204 500, Spanien, Schlacht um Guadalajara

combatants: Nationalist Spain and Italian Corpo Truppe Volontarie
details.gloss: Italian CTV and Nationalist troops advanced northeast of Madrid but were checked by Republican counterattacks. The Republican victory dented Italian prestige and kept pressure off the capital.

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Brunete: Battle of Brunete

details.gloss: The Republicans attacked west of Madrid to relieve pressure on the north. Initial gains collapsed under Nationalist counterattacks, costing both sides heavily.

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Teruel: Republican Soldiers in the Battle of Teruel Google Art Project

details.gloss: Republican forces captured Teruel during a brutal winter offensive, only for Nationalists to retake the city. The battle bled both armies and exposed Republican limits.

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of the Ebro: Batalla Ebro

combatants: Nationalist Spain and Axis air forces
details.gloss: Republican armies crossed the Ebro in their last major offensive, delaying but not reversing Nationalist momentum. Their defeat opened the road to Catalonia.

image.alt: Historical image associated with Siege of Detroit: Reddition de Détroit

details.gloss: British and Indigenous forces under Isaac Brock and Tecumseh compelled William Hull to surrender Detroit without a major assault. The victory secured Upper Canada early in the War of 1812.

image.alt: Historical image associated with Burning of Washington: Capture of the city of Washington LCCN2007683564 (cropped)
image.attribution: J. Cundee, publisher; Library of Congress

details.gloss: British troops entered Washington, D.C., after the American defeat at Bladensburg and burned major public buildings. The raid shocked the United States but did not end the war.

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Baltimore: Ft. Henry bombardement 1814
image.attribution: Unknown artist; original uploader Dr.frog at English Wikipedia

details.gloss: American defenders repelled the British attack on Baltimore and withstood the bombardment of Fort McHenry. Francis Scott Key's view of the flag inspired the poem that became the national anthem, "The Star-Spangled Banner."

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Copenhagen (1801): The Battle of Copenhagen, 2 April 1801 RMG BHC0529

details.gloss: Nelson led a hard-fought attack on the Dano-Norwegian line at Copenhagen. The British victory helped break the League of Armed Neutrality in the Baltic.
details.casualties: Royal Navy: 274 killed and 737 wounded; Denmark-Norway: estimates vary, including 367 killed, 635 wounded, and 1,179 captured

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Copenhagen (1807): Copenhagen on fire 1807 by CW Eckersberg

details.gloss: British forces bombarded Copenhagen to seize the Dano-Norwegian fleet before it could fall under French influence. The attack pushed Denmark-Norway into war against Britain.
details.casualties: Britain: 42 killed and 145 wounded; Denmark-Norway: military casualties plus about 195 civilians killed and 768 civilians injured

image.alt: Historical image associated with Mystic massacre: Mystic Massacre in New England 1638 Photo Facsimile
image.attribution: John Underhill; photo-facsimile by Edward Bierstadt

combatants: Connecticut Colony, Narragansett, and Mohegan allies
details.gloss: English colonial troops and Narragansett and Mohegan allies burned the fortified Pequot village at Mystic. The massacre broke Pequot power and became a defining event of the Pequot War.
details.casualties: Pequot: about 400-700 killed; Connecticut, Narragansett, and Mohegan forces: light losses

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Bloody Brook: Bloody Brook Monument South Deerfield, MA DSC07422

combatants: New England Confederation and Mohegan allies
details.gloss: Native forces ambushed a colonial wagon train and its escorts near South Deerfield. Bloody Brook became one of the sharp early shocks of King Philip's War.
details.casualties: Pocumtuc-led Native forces: unknown; New England Confederation and Mohegan allies: at least 58 militia and 16 civilians killed

image.alt: Historical image associated with Great Swamp Fight: Great Swamp Fight painting
image.attribution: Ancient and Honorable Artillery Company of Massachusetts

combatants: New England Confederation and Native allies
details.gloss: Colonial militia and Native allies assaulted a Narragansett winter fort in the Great Swamp. The attack caused catastrophic Narragansett losses and widened King Philip's War.
details.casualties: New England Confederation and Native allies: about 70 killed and 150 wounded; Narragansett: about 97 warriors and 300-1,000 noncombatants killed, 300 captured

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Bushy Run: The Battle of Bushy Run

combatants: Delaware, Shawnee, Mingo, and Huron forces
details.gloss: Henry Bouquet's British column fought through Native attacks while moving to relieve Fort Pitt. The victory helped stabilize British positions during Pontiac's War.
details.casualties: Delaware, Shawnee, Mingo, and Huron forces: about 20-60 killed; Great Britain: about 50 killed and 60 wounded

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Fallen Timbers: Fallen timbers

details.gloss: Anthony Wayne's Legion defeated the Northwestern Confederacy near a storm-felled woodland. Fallen Timbers led to the Treaty of Greenville and opened much of Ohio to U.S. settlement.
details.casualties: United States: 33 killed and 100 wounded; Northwestern Confederacy: about 25-40 killed

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Tippecanoe: Battle of Tippecanoe LCCN2003656861

details.gloss: Forces associated with Tenskwatawa attacked William Henry Harrison's camp near Prophetstown. The battle damaged Tecumseh's confederacy and shaped the politics leading into the War of 1812.
details.casualties: Tecumseh's Confederacy: about 36-65 killed, 70-80 wounded, and 1 captured; United States: 62 killed and 126 wounded

image.alt: Historical image associated with Sand Creek massacre: At the Sand Creek Massacre, 1874 1875

details.gloss: Colorado volunteers under John Chivington attacked a Cheyenne and Arapaho village at Sand Creek. The massacre became one of the most notorious atrocities of the American Indian Wars.
details.casualties: United States: 24 killed and 52 wounded; Cheyenne and Arapaho: estimates range from about 70 to 600 killed

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of the Washita River: Seventh Cavalry Charging Black Kettle s Village 1868

details.outcome: United States victory; contested as a massacre
details.gloss: Custer's 7th Cavalry attacked Black Kettle's Southern Cheyenne camp on the Washita River. The action remains disputed as both a battle and a massacre.
details.casualties: United States: 21 killed and 13 wounded; Cheyenne: estimates range from 13 to 150 killed, with 53 women and children captured

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of the Rosebud: Sioux charging at Battle of Rosebud

combatants: United States, Crow, and Shoshoni allies
details.gloss: Lakota and Cheyenne warriors under leaders including Crazy Horse checked George Crook's column at Rosebud Creek. The fight kept Crook from joining the campaign that soon led to Little Bighorn.
details.casualties: Lakota, Cheyenne, and Arapaho: about 13-36 killed and 63-100 wounded; United States, Crow, and Shoshoni: about 15-36 killed and 43-46 wounded

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Bear Paw: Bear Paw Battlefield

details.gloss: U.S. troops caught the Nez Perce near the Canadian border after a long retreat. Chief Joseph's surrender at Bear Paw ended the Nez Perce War.
details.casualties: United States: 24 dead and 49 wounded; Nez Perce: 25 killed, 46 wounded, and 431 surrendered or captured

image.alt: Historical image associated with Wounded Knee Massacre: Battle of Wounded Knee 14867u

details.gloss: U.S. 7th Cavalry troops killed Lakota men, women, and children near Wounded Knee Creek. The massacre marked the violent close of the major U.S. Army campaigns against Plains peoples.
details.casualties: United States: 25-31 killed and 33-39 wounded; Lakota: about 250-300 killed and 51 wounded

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Yatay: Combat Du Yatay (rive droit de l'Uruguay)

details.location: Yatay Creek near Paso de los Libres, Argentina
details.gloss: Triple Alliance forces defeated a Paraguayan column on the Uruguay River. Yatay was the first major land battle of the Paraguayan War and helped reverse Paraguay's early offensive.
details.casualties: Triple Alliance: 147 killed and 560 wounded; Paraguay: 1,700 killed, 300 wounded, and 1,200 captured

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Curupayty: Cándido Lopez Trinchera de Curupaytí Google Art Project (497159)

details.gloss: Paraguayan defenders used prepared trenches and artillery to repulse Allied frontal assaults. Curupayty was a severe Triple Alliance setback and slowed the Humaita campaign.
details.casualties: Paraguay: 54 killed and 38 wounded; Triple Alliance: 2,225 Argentine casualties and 2,002 Brazilian casualties

image.alt: Historical image associated with Siege of Humaitá: Bird's eye of Humaitá

details.gloss: Allied forces besieged the fortress complex guarding the Paraguay River until the Paraguayan garrison evacuated and surrendered. The fall of Humaitá opened the route toward Asuncion.

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Lomas Valentinas: Lomas Vallentinas

details.gloss: Allied armies defeated Francisco Solano Lopez's main remaining field force during the Pikysyry campaign. The victory opened the road to Asuncion and forced Lopez deeper into the interior.
details.casualties: Paraguay: 1,500 killed and 1,500 captured; Triple Alliance: 3,270 casualties

image.alt: Historical image associated with Battle of Acosta Ñu: Batalha de Campo Grande 1871

details.gloss: Brazilian and Argentine forces destroyed a Paraguayan force that included many boys late in the war. The battle is remembered in Paraguay as the Children's Battle.
details.casualties: Paraguay: 2,000 killed and 1,500 wounded or captured; Brazil and Argentina: 182 killed and 420 wounded
